questions.prompt: A product owner writes "Checkout redesign" as a months-long initiative that several smaller pieces of work will roll up under. Which Jira issue type fits best?
options: Bug
Story
Epic
Test run
questions.explanation: An epic is a large body of work spanning many stories over a long period, which is exactly what a multi-week initiative made of smaller pieces is.

questions.prompt: You execute the same test case in three different sprints and get pass, then fail, then pass. Where do these three different outcomes live?
options: In the test case itself, which is overwritten each time
In three separate test runs, each recording the result against its build
In the RTM, which stores per-execution results
In the bug ticket's workflow history
questions.explanation: The case defines reusable steps; each execution and its result is recorded in a separate test run against a specific build, so one case can have many independent outcomes.

questions.prompt: During a run, a case cannot be executed because the test data it needs has not been created. How should you mark it?
options: Pass, since nothing failed
Fail, and raise a bug
Blocked, with a note explaining why
Skip it silently and move on
questions.explanation: A case that could not run is Blocked, not Pass. Marking it Blocked with a note keeps the run honest and flags that coverage is incomplete.

questions.prompt: TC-08 fails: an expired discount code still applies. What is the main reason to link the new bug to the failed test run rather than filing it on its own?
options: It automatically fixes the workflow state of the story
It creates traceability so the bug shows the exact case, steps, and build that failed
It is required before any code can be deployed
It moves the test case to the Done column on the board
questions.explanation: Linking establishes traceability: the bug carries the exact failing case, reproduction, and build, and the run shows what is blocking sign-off.

questions.prompt: While reviewing your RTM you find a requirement that has no test cases mapped to it. What does this most directly tell you?
options: That requirement has an open bug
That requirement is fully tested and passing
That requirement is a coverage gap with no verification
That requirement should be deleted from scope
questions.explanation: An RTM maps requirements to the cases that verify them. A requirement with no linked cases is an untested coverage gap, not a passing or buggy one.
